summary:
The Capsian culture was a North African Epipaleolithic and early Neolithic tradition centered in the Maghreb region — particularly Algeria and Tunisia — dated to approximately 10,000–6,000 BCE. Named after the site of Capsa (modern Gafsa in Tunisia), the culture is recognized by its distinctive microlithic stone tool industry and by the characteristic escargotieres — enormous middens composed primarily of land snail shells (Helix species) mixed with animal bones, stone tools, ostrich eggshell fragments, and other occupational debris.

The escargotieres of the Capsian culture reveal a subsistence economy heavily dependent on land snails as a food source, supplemented by hunting of aurochs, Barbary sheep, hartebeest, and other game animals, alongside gathering of wild plant foods. The Capsian people produced a relatively elaborate material culture including ground stone tools, bone tools, and personal ornaments. Capsian mortuary practices included both inhumation and secondary burial. The relationship between the Capsian culture and the earlier Ibero-Maurusian (Oranian) culture of the same region, and between Capsian populations and later Neolithic and Berber groups, remains an area of active research. The Capsian culture flourished during the [[event:17|African Humid Period]] when North Africa was considerably more habitable than today.
